one_line_takeaway: By decomposing 32 jailbreak papers into a compact, LLM-interpreted dictionary of ~397 reusable adversarial skill primitives via sparse dictionary learning, and training LLMs on diverse compositions of those primitives (ASCoT) rather than isolated attacks, the authors cut StrongReject harmfulness on six held-out unseen jailbreak families to near-zero while keeping over-refusal in check, evidence that unseen jailbreaks are recompositions of a finite skill space rather than fundamentally novel threats.
key_finding: Despite 35x compression (14,070 raw skills to 397 dictionary atoms), Dfinal matches the full overcomplete dictionary Dover in mean explainability (delta < 0.05) on unseen attacks, e.g. GPT-4.1-judged explainability scores of 4.21-4.69 (Dfinal) vs. 4.23-4.72 (Dover) across the six unseen attack families, while requiring far fewer active primitives (mean sparsity ~5.9-7.5 for Dfinal vs. ~12.1-13.3 for Dover). Explanatory power increases monotonically with cutoff date across 5 temporal cutoffs, plateauing around 4.3-4.4/5. On defense, ASCoT achieves near-zero StrongReject harmfulness on unseen attacks in Table 2 (e.g., LLaMA-3.1-8B ASCoT(closed): 0.11 mean unseen-attack harm score vs. 0.30-0.71 for No Defense/Refusal Training/CAT/LAT/WildJailbreak baselines) while keeping over-refusal moderate (0.12 vs. 0.06-0.98 range across baselines), and an 8B ASCoT-tuned LLaMA model roughly matches Claude Sonnet-4-Thinking and outperforms OpenAI o4-mini on several StrongReject jailbreak families (Table 3). A controlled ablation shows ASCoT scores harmfulness 0.00 across all tested novel (untrained) compositions of k=2..5 primitives, versus 0.59-0.69 for the undefended base model (Table 4). A 'coverage dividend' is observed: expanding skill coverage (12/50/200/all primitives) at fixed training-data size steadily reduces harmfulness against both PAIR and AutoDAN-Turbo, and shallow (k=1,2) vs. deep (k=4,5) compositional-depth training specializes defenses toward short (~102 tokens/~9.7 skills, PAIR) vs. long (~335 tokens/~14.8 skills, AutoDAN-Turbo) attacks respectively.
core_contribution: The paper proposes the 'Adversarial Deja Vu' hypothesis -- that novel LLM jailbreaks are largely sparse recombinations of adversarial 'skills' (transferable prompt-transformation techniques) seen in earlier attacks -- and tests it with an automated pipeline: extract skills from 32 jailbreak papers (Nov 2022-Nov 2024, 1,494 original/mutated prompt pairs, 16,901 raw extracted skills) via GPT-4.1, embed skill explanations with text-embedding-3-large (d=3072), compress them into a sparse 'Jailbreak Dictionary' via K-SVD dictionary learning (Aharon et al. 2006) with a Lasso/LARS sparse-coding step, and interpret dictionary atoms via LLM-augmented basis-pursuit denoising (BPDN) against the raw-skill matrix. Building on the finding that unseen (post-cutoff) jailbreaks are explainable as sparse compositions of these primitives, the authors introduce Adversarial Skill Compositional Training (ASCoT): a data-centric defense that fine-tunes LLMs on harmful queries mutated by composing k in {1..5} sampled dictionary primitives (via DeepSeek-V3-Chat as the composing/rewriting model), rather than on isolated attack instances.
threat_model: Language-only, single-turn jailbreak prompts against open-weight instruction-tuned LLMs (LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, Zephyr-7B-Beta, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2). The adversary crafts natural-language prompts that recombine known adversarial 'skills' (e.g., academic framing, role-play, persuasion, obfuscation) to elicit harmful outputs; the paper explicitly scopes out attacks requiring internal model access (e.g., fine-tuning attacks) and, in its main study, multi-turn dialogue attacks (though it separately tests transfer to one multi-turn attack, GALA). Evaluated attack families include seen attacks (Direct/StrongReject, GCG, PAIR, BEAST, Simple Adaptive Attack) and unseen/held-out attacks (AutoDAN-Turbo, DarkCite, Emoji, FlipAttack, Implicit Reference, SequentialBreak, and multi-turn GALA), with a temporal cutoff of August 15, 2024 separating 26 seen jailbreak papers from 6 unseen ones.
limitations: The paper itself notes: the sparse-linear-composition assumption in embedding space is 'a working modeling choice for tractability and interpretability, not a formal proof of linear composition'; the study is scoped to single-turn, language-only jailbreaks and explicitly excludes attacks requiring internal model access (e.g., fine-tuning attacks); skill extraction, dictionary naming, and explainability scoring all rely on LLM judges (GPT-4.1, cross-checked with Claude 3.7 Sonnet) rather than human annotation, though the authors do use a second judge to mitigate self-evaluation bias; the temporal cutoff (Aug 15, 2024) was deliberately chosen to balance seen-set size against unseen diversity, which could affect generalizability of the plateau finding; ASCoT's compositional training data itself is generated by an auxiliary LLM (DeepSeek-V3-Chat) chosen partly for weak alignment/high compliance, meaning defense quality depends on that generator's coverage and fidelity; the paper acknowledges future work is needed to extend the framework to multi-turn and reasoning-driven attacks and cross-modal (vision/speech) adversarial skill taxonomies.

datasets_used: 32 curated jailbreak papers (Nov 17, 2022 - Nov 18, 2024), 1,494 original-mutated prompt pairs, 16,901 extracted raw skills
PKU-SafeRLHF (vanilla harmful queries and base prompts for skill-composed queries)
XSTest (over-refusal calibration and 125-query held-out ORR evaluation)
quantitative_results: Skill extraction: 1,494 prompt pairs -> 16,901 raw skills; 35x compression to kfinal=397 dictionary primitives

Explainability scores (GPT-4.1 judge, Dfinal vs Dover): AutoDAN-Turbo 4.21 vs 4.23; DarkCite 4.26 vs 4.30; Emoji 4.30 vs 4.30; FlipAttack 4.32 vs 4.37; Implicit Reference 4.69 vs 4.72; SequentialBreak 4.36 vs 4.37
Sparsity (mean active primitives per unseen skill), Dfinal vs Dover: AutoDAN-Turbo 5.90 vs 12.11; SequentialBreak 6.54 vs 12.60; FlipAttack 7.46 vs 12.27

Table 2, LLaMA-3.1-8B mean unseen-attack StrongReject harm score: No Defense 0.71 (Seen)/varies, Refusal Training 0.30, CAT 0.50, LAT 0.39, WildJailbreak 0.35, ASCoT(closed) 0.11, ASCoT(open) 0.11; XSTest ORR: No Defense 0.06, ASCoT(closed) 0.12, ASCoT(open) 0.06
Table 3, StrongReject mean harmful score: o4-mini 0.19, Claude Sonnet-4-Thinking 0.08, LLaMA-3.1-8B ASCoT(open) 0.08, with ORR 0.26, 0.04, 0.06 respectively
Table 4, novel-composition harmfulness (k=2,3,4,5): base LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct 0.62/0.61/0.59/0.69 vs ASCoT 0.00/0.00/0.00/0.00
Table 5, average tokens/skills per query: PAIR ~102 tokens/~9.7 skills; AutoDAN-Turbo ~335 tokens/~14.8 skills
ASCoT training dataset size: 40,526 examples; skill-coverage ablation datasets: 38k examples each at coverage levels 12/50/200/all primitives (12 clusters)
baselines_compared: CAT (Xhonneux et al. 2024) and re-implemented CAT* (matched to ~40k examples)
LAT (Sheshadri et al. 2024) and re-implemented LAT* (matched to ~40k examples)
OpenAI o4-mini and Claude Sonnet-4-Thinking (closed-source reasoning models, Table 3 comparison)
